learning_objectives: Define digital communications and distinguish it from traditional media
Identify the major platforms and channels in the current digital ecosystem
Trace the historical evolution from print and broadcast to digital-first communication
readings: Pew Research Center, 'State of Digital Media' (latest annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to Digital Communications. This course provides the foundation for everything you will study in the Digital Communications program, covering the platforms, principles, and practices that define how organizations and individuals communicate in a networked world.
key_concepts: Digital communications defined: the creation, distribution, and management of messages through digital channels
The shift from one-to-many broadcasting to many-to-many networked communication
Key platforms: social media, websites, email, messaging apps, podcasts, video streaming
Convergence: how formerly separate media (text, audio, video) now coexist on single platforms
The attention economy: competing for audience engagement in a content-saturated environment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side comparison of how the same news story is communicated across print, broadcast TV, a news website, Twitter/X, TikTok, and a podcast, highlighting differences in format, tone, and audience engagement
setup_requirements: Access to major social media platforms for observation (accounts not required for posting)
Notebook or digital document for the semester-long media diary

review.value: The merging of previously distinct media forms (text, audio, video, interactive) into unified digital platforms, enabling multi-format storytelling
The framework recognizing that human attention is a scarce resource and that digital communicators compete for finite audience engagement

class_schedule.topic: Audiences and Engagement in Digital Spaces
learning_objectives: Distinguish between reach, impressions, and engagement as audience metrics
Explain how digital audiences differ from traditional media audiences in behavior and expectations
Analyze audience demographics and psychographics for a given digital platform

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding your audience is the first principle of effective communication. In digital spaces, audiences are not passive receivers but active participants who share, comment, remix, and create alongside you.
key_concepts: Active vs. passive audiences: how digital platforms transform consumption into participation
Reach vs. impressions vs. engagement: what each metric measures and when each matters
Demographic segmentation: age, location, income, education as audience descriptors
Psychographic segmentation: values, interests, attitudes, and lifestyle as deeper audience understanding
Platform-audience alignment: matching message format and tone to platform norms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using publicly available analytics dashboards (YouTube Studio demo account, Twitter Analytics examples) to interpret real audience data and identify engagement patterns
setup_requirements: Access to sample analytics dashboards (provided on LMS)

review.value: The ratio of audience interactions (likes, comments, shares, clicks) to total reach or impressions, measuring how compelling your content is to those who see it
Dividing audiences by psychological characteristics such as values, interests, and attitudes, which often predict content preferences better than demographics alone

learning_objectives: Define content strategy and explain its role in digital communications
Identify the components of a basic content strategy: goals, audience, channels, content types, and cadence
Evaluate an existing organization's content strategy for coherence and effectiveness
readings: Halvorson & Rach, Content Strategy for the Web, Ch. 1-2
Content Marketing Institute, 'What Is Content Strategy?' (online guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Content strategy is the planning, creation, delivery, and governance of useful, usable content. Without strategy, content creation becomes reactive and fragmented. With it, every piece of content serves a purpose.
key_concepts: Content strategy defined: the plan for creating, publishing, and managing content that achieves specific goals
Strategic alignment: connecting content decisions to organizational objectives
Content audit: inventorying existing content to identify gaps, redundancies, and opportunities
Editorial calendar: planning content production across channels and time
Content governance: who creates, approves, publishes, and maintains content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live content audit of a university department's website and social media, mapping content to stated goals and identifying strategic gaps

review.value: A systematic inventory of all existing content across channels, evaluating each piece for quality, relevance, accuracy, and alignment with strategic goals
A planning document scheduling content creation and publication across channels, ensuring consistent cadence and strategic coverage of topics

learning_objectives: Explain why narrative is effective in digital communications
Apply basic storytelling frameworks to digital content creation
Adapt a single story across multiple digital formats and platforms
readings: Alexander, The New Digital Storytelling, Ch. 1-3
StoryBrand framework overview (Donald Miller, online summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Humans are wired for stories. Even in professional and organizational communications, narrative structures increase comprehension, retention, and emotional connection. Digital platforms offer new tools for telling stories but demand adaptation of traditional narrative techniques.
key_concepts: Narrative arc in digital content: setup, tension, resolution adapted for short-form and interactive media
The StoryBrand framework: positioning the audience as the hero and the brand as the guide
Transmedia storytelling: extending a narrative across multiple platforms with unique contributions from each
Micro-narratives: telling complete stories in tweets, Instagram captions, or 60-second videos
User-generated narrative: inviting audiences to contribute to and shape the story
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing a successful brand campaign (Dove Real Beauty or Patagonia's environmental storytelling) across all its digital touchpoints, mapping each piece to the narrative arc
setup_requirements: Access to campaign case study materials (provided on LMS)

review.value: A narrative technique where different elements of a story are delivered across multiple platforms, each contributing uniquely to the whole rather than simply repeating the same content
A messaging framework where the customer is the hero with a problem, and the brand is the guide offering a plan that leads to success and helps avoid failure

learning_objectives: Distinguish between vanity metrics and actionable metrics
Set up a basic measurement framework using KPIs aligned to communication goals
Interpret a Google Analytics overview report to extract meaningful insights
readings: Google Analytics Academy, 'Digital Analytics Fundamentals' Module 1

lecture_notes.introduction: If you cannot measure it, you cannot improve it. Digital communications offers unprecedented measurement capability compared to traditional media, but only if you know what to measure and why.
key_concepts: Vanity metrics vs. actionable metrics: followers and likes vs. conversion rates and engagement quality
KPI framework: Key Performance Indicators tied directly to communication objectives
The measurement funnel: awareness, engagement, conversion, advocacy
Attribution: determining which touchpoints contributed to a desired outcome
A/B testing fundamentals: isolating variables to determine what works
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a Google Analytics demo account to identify traffic sources, user behavior flow, and conversion paths, then translating findings into actionable recommendations
setup_requirements: Google Analytics demo account access (provided by Google)

review.value: Metrics like follower counts or page views that look impressive but do not directly indicate whether communication goals are being achieved
A framework for assigning credit to different touchpoints in a customer journey, determining which channels and content contributed to a desired outcome

class_schedule.topic: Ethics and Responsibility in Digital Communications
learning_objectives: Identify the primary ethical challenges facing digital communicators
Apply ethical frameworks to real-world digital communications dilemmas
Evaluate the tension between persuasion and manipulation in digital marketing
readings: Parsons, Ethics in Public Relations, Ch. 8 - Digital Ethics
PRSA Code of Ethics (online, digital communications provisions)

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital communicators hold significant power to shape public perception, influence behavior, and spread information. With that power comes ethical responsibility that goes beyond legal compliance.
key_concepts: Transparency: disclosing sponsored content, affiliations, and data collection practices
Authenticity vs. manipulation: where persuasion ends and deception begins
Data privacy: ethical collection, use, and protection of audience data
Misinformation responsibility: the communicator's duty to verify before amplifying
Accessibility: ensuring digital content is usable by people with disabilities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical case study analysis: examining three real-world digital communications controversies (astroturfing, undisclosed sponsorship, data misuse) using the Potter Box ethical framework

review.value: A four-step ethical reasoning framework: define the situation, identify values, apply principles, choose loyalties, used to systematically analyze ethical dilemmas in communications
The ethical obligation to clearly disclose the nature, source, and intent of communications, including sponsored content, data collection, and organizational affiliations

questions.question: Explain the difference between reach and engagement, and describe a scenario where a campaign could have high reach but low engagement. What would this indicate about the content?
questions.answer: Reach measures how many unique users saw the content, while engagement measures interactions such as likes, comments, shares, and clicks. A campaign could have high reach but low engagement if, for example, a promoted post is shown to a broad audience through paid distribution but the content does not resonate with that audience. This would indicate a mismatch between the content and the target audience, poor creative quality, or ineffective calls to action.

questions.question: What is digital convergence, and how has it changed the way organizations communicate with their audiences?
questions.answer: Digital convergence is the merging of previously separate media forms (text, audio, video, interactive elements) onto unified digital platforms. It has changed organizational communication by enabling multi-format storytelling on single platforms (e.g., a single Instagram post can combine image, text caption, video, and interactive polls), by collapsing the distinction between content producers and consumers, and by requiring communicators to master multiple media forms rather than specializing in one.

rubric.key: Identifies specific impacts on organizational communication

questions.question: Define content strategy and list its five core components. Then explain why an editorial calendar is essential rather than optional.
questions.answer: Content strategy is the planning, creation, delivery, and governance of content to achieve specific organizational goals. Its five core components are: goals (what the content should achieve), audience (who it serves), channels (where it is distributed), content types (what formats are used), and cadence (how often content is published). An editorial calendar is essential because without it, content production becomes reactive, inconsistent in frequency, and prone to gaps in topic coverage, leading to audience attrition and misalignment with strategic goals.

rubric.key: Explains editorial calendar necessity with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the StoryBrand framework and describe how a nonprofit organization could use it to structure a fundraising campaign message.
questions.answer: The StoryBrand framework positions the audience as the hero of the story and the brand as the guide. The hero has a problem, the guide offers a plan, calls the hero to action, and the result is either success or failure avoidance. A nonprofit could use this by positioning the donor as the hero who wants to make a difference (the desire), faces the obstacle of not knowing how to help effectively (the problem), is guided by the nonprofit's specific program (the plan), is asked to donate a specific amount (the call to action), and is shown the tangible impact of their gift (success) versus the consequences of inaction (failure).

rubric.key: Applies it correctly to a nonprofit scenario
Includes both success and failure outcomes

major_assignments.title: Digital Communications Audit
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive audit of a real organization's digital communications presence across all major channels. Evaluate coherence, effectiveness, audience alignment, and strategic gaps, then provide actionable recommendations for improvement.
objectives: Inventory an organization's digital presence across all active channels
Evaluate content quality, consistency, and alignment with apparent organizational goals
Identify audience engagement patterns and platform-specific performance
Deliver actionable recommendations grounded in course frameworks
deliverables: Written audit report (8-10 pages) covering all active digital channels
Content inventory spreadsheet cataloging at least 50 pieces of content
Competitive comparison with two peer organizations
Recommendations document with prioritized action items

criteria: All active digital channels are identified and analyzed
Content inventory is comprehensive with consistent categorization
Both quantitative metrics and qualitative assessment are included
Analysis applies course frameworks (content strategy, audience segmentation, engagement metrics)
Findings are supported by specific evidence from the audit
Strengths and weaknesses are identified with equal rigor
Patterns and themes are drawn from individual observations
Recommendations are specific, actionable, and prioritized
Each recommendation is tied to a finding from the audit
Recommendations are realistic for the organization's apparent resources
Quick wins and long-term strategic changes are both addressed
Comprehensive audit demonstrating systematic analysis across all channels
Strong analytical quality applying course concepts to real-world findings
Actionable, evidence-based recommendations prioritized by impact
Professional writing and presentation quality throughout

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes assessing comprehension of digital communications concepts

participation_and_media_diary.description: Class participation and weekly media diary entries documenting digital communications observations

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive digital communications audit of a real organization

midterm_examination.description: Written examination covering all concepts from the first half of the course

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination assessing mastery of all course material

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction. Active participation in discussions is part of the grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. Class discussions and peer feedback sessions encourage collaboration, but all submitted work must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All sources must be properly cited. Presenting others' work as your own, including AI-generated content without disclosure, constitutes academic dishonesty and will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with internet access, a web browser, and access to Google Analytics demo account. Social media accounts on major platforms are recommended for observation purposes.
